tasks.description: Find top academic papers on a well-defined AI topic with multi-database search and citation analysis
tasks.input: Find the top 5 most influential recent papers on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for reducing hallucination in large language models. I need papers from 2023 or later, with citation counts, publication venues, and a brief synthesis of how the field is evolving. Prioritize peer-reviewed work but include significant preprints.

scoring.5: Constructs database-specific queries for arXiv (using cat:cs.CL, ti:/abs: prefixes), Semantic Scholar (with fieldsOfStudy, year filter, citation fields), and Google Scholar; uses appropriate academic terminology (RAG, hallucination, LLM, faithfulness)
scoring.3: Searches 2 databases with reasonable queries but misses database-specific optimizations (e.g., no category codes on arXiv)
scoring.1: Searches only one database or uses generic web-search-style queries
scoring.0: No academic database search attempted; treats as a general web search

scoring.5: All 5 papers directly address RAG for hallucination reduction; includes mix of foundational work and recent advances; papers are from reputable venues (ACL, EMNLP, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, or high-citation arXiv preprints)
scoring.3: 3-4 papers are directly relevant; 1-2 are tangentially related (e.g., general RAG without hallucination focus, or general hallucination without RAG)
scoring.1: Fewer than 3 relevant papers; includes non-academic sources or off-topic papers
scoring.0: Results are not academic papers or do not address the topic

scoring.5: Each paper includes: full title, authors, year, venue, publication status (peer-reviewed/preprint), citation count, DOI or arXiv ID, and open-access link; citation velocity or influential citation count noted
scoring.3: Papers have title, authors, and year but missing venue, citation data, or identifiers for some entries
scoring.1: Only titles and URLs provided; no citation analysis

scoring.5: Provides a synthesis connecting the 5 papers: identifies thematic clusters (e.g., retrieval methods, evaluation approaches), notes consensus findings, highlights open questions, and suggests a reading order
scoring.3: Brief summary of each paper but no cross-paper synthesis or thematic grouping
scoring.1: Raw list of papers with abstracts but no synthesis
scoring.0: No synthesis or narrative connecting results
